skill.name: devflow-file-standards
skill.description: Defines shared CC-DevFlow file naming, directory layout, YAML frontmatter, status files, and contract file conventions.
skill.summary: Standardizes CC-DevFlow files, folders, frontmatter, status schemas, and contract naming conventions.
skill.author: Dimon94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, sensitive-file, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples, file names, regex patterns, or gitignore entries. The skill contains documentation only, with no executable code, network access, secret reads, destructive commands, or prompt-injection language.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks and File Paths Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code fences, inline file paths, command names used as documentation, and example directory trees. No shell execution, script body, or command invocation is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown examples and inline references. There is no executable code or user input path that could trigger command execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Sensitive File Pattern Documents Gitignore Rules
low_findings.description: The .env and secrets entries are listed under files that should not be committed. This documents exclusion rules and does not read, print, copy, or exfiltrate sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a gitignore pattern block that recommends excluding .env, secrets, and node_modules. It is defensive documentation, not secret access.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Security and Status Words Matched Malware Heuristics
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and weak cryptography findings are caused by benign words inside documentation, such as SECURITY_REPORT.md, status values, and standards text. No cryptographic algorithm, Windows database path, or credential extraction logic is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious matches are ordinary documentation labels and status names. Review found no code implementing weak hashes or accessing Windows SAM data.

content.value_statement: DevFlow projects can drift when files, folders, IDs, and status documents use inconsistent formats. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code one reference for shared CC-DevFlow file standards.
actual_capabilities: Defines REQ-ID and task ID formats for CC-DevFlow workflows.
Documents the expected requirements and bug directory structures.
Lists YAML frontmatter fields for PRD, TECH_DESIGN, EPIC, and TASKS files.
Explains orchestration_status.json and bug status.json field conventions.
Describes naming patterns for Markdown documents, scripts, directories, and contracts.
Identifies which file standards belong outside agent-specific guidance.
limitations: It does not validate files automatically or run repository checks.
It does not include full CC-DevFlow templates or agent execution standards.
It assumes projects already follow the CC-DevFlow workflow structure.
It documents implicit conventions that may need local confirmation in customized setups.

use_cases.title: Create a New Requirement Folder
use_cases.description: Use the documented REQ-ID, folder layout, and required document names before starting a new feature flow.

use_cases.title: Review Workflow Artifacts
use_cases.description: Check whether PRD, TECH_DESIGN, EPIC, TASKS, and status files use the expected names and frontmatter fields.

use_cases.title: Align Automation Outputs
use_cases.description: Reference shared file standards when updating scripts, agents, or commands that generate DevFlow artifacts.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Requirement ID
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the DevFlow file standards, check whether this requirement ID is valid: REQ-042.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Requirement Directory
prompt_templates.prompt: List the expected files and folders for a new CC-DevFlow requirement named REQ-123.

prompt_templates.title: Review Frontmatter Fields
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the frontmatter fields in my PRD, TECH_DESIGN, EPIC, and TASKS files against the DevFlow standards.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Workflow Consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this DevFlow artifact list for naming, directory structure, status schema, and contract file convention issues.

output_examples.input: Where should PRD.md be stored for REQ-123?
output_examples.output: Place it in the requirement folder at devflow/requirements/REQ-123/PRD.md.
It should include reqId, totalTasks, completedTasks, version, createdAt, updatedAt, and author.

output_examples.input: How should an OpenAPI contract for users be named?
output_examples.output: Use the api-resource pattern. For users, the documented example is api-users.yaml.

best_practices: Use this skill as the shared reference before creating or reviewing DevFlow artifacts.
Keep agent-specific content rules in their owning agents and use this skill only for format standards.
Check generated files against the documented names, locations, and frontmatter before handoff.
anti_patterns: Do not add detailed PRD, architecture, UI, planning, or QA methodology to this skill.
Do not commit .env files, secrets directories, or node_modules under DevFlow requirement folders.
Do not invent alternate status names or ID formats without updating the shared standards.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands or validate files?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation reference and does not include executable validation logic.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What requirement ID format does it define?
faq.answer: It defines REQ followed by a hyphen and three digits, such as REQ-001.

faq.question: What task ID format does it define?
faq.answer: It defines T followed by three digits, such as T001.

faq.question: Does it include full document templates?
faq.answer: No. It references template locations but focuses on shared file and format standards.

faq.question: Can it help with bug workflow files?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents the expected bug folder layout and status.json fields.
